summary: Jeff Atwood introduces the LinkTron 5000, a tool he built to automatically determine which words on a web page would generate unique Google search results. This follows his earlier concept of 'unbreakable links' - expressing URLs as search queries rather than absolute addresses to prevent link rot. The tool involves significant Google querying but uses caching for performance. He credits Philipp Lenssen's Google Blogoscoped as a key resource, particularly for word frequency data.
key_points: The LinkTron 5000 analyzes web pages to find words that would produce unique Google search results
The tool implements the 'unbreakable links' concept from a previous post - using search queries instead of absolute URLs
Heavy Google querying is mitigated through pre-caching, though unusual words may slow processing
Word frequency analysis is central to determining which terms are distinctive enough for unique searches
money_quotes: Great concept, but how do you determine which words on a web page are most likely to generate a unique search result?
As you might imagine, this involves quite a bit of Google abuse – all of which is pre-cached for performance.
key_insight: By analyzing word frequency and uniqueness, you can automatically identify which terms on a page would serve as reliable search-based alternatives to fragile direct URLs.
